Parliamentary Inquiry Text Mining Report¶

Author: Juan Vargas T.

Email: juan.vargastorres@student.uts.edu.au

Website: jsebastianv.com

Repository: github.com/Sebas102507/nlp-parliamentary-inquiry-text-mining

Table of Contents¶

Use the links below to jump to each section.

  • 0. Utilities
    • 0.0 Environment setup
    • 0.1 Imports and package setup
    • 0.2 Global constants and visual theme
    • 0.3 Data models and metadata parsing classes
    • 0.4 Corpus ingestion service class
    • 0.5 Text preprocessing class and helper functions
  • 1. Introduction
  • 2. Data Ingestion
  • 3. Exploratory Data Analysis
    • 3.1 Corpus overview visuals
    • 3.2 Corpus composition by submitter type
    • 3.3 Distribution of document word counts
    • 3.4 Relationship between pages and word counts
    • 3.5 Character counts by submitter type
    • 3.6 Linguistic annotation sample (NER and POS)
  • 4. Preprocessing
    • 4.1 Prepare cleaned tokens and lexical metrics
    • 4.2 Most common words
    • 4.3 Corpus snapshot statistics
    • 4.4 Frequency table preparation (unigrams and NPMI bigrams)
    • 4.5 Top unigram frequency chart
    • 4.6 Bigram phrase visuals (frequency chart and network graph)
    • 4.7 Top three-gram frequency chart
    • 4.8 Word cloud of preprocessed corpus
  • 5. Advanced Methods (Topic Modeling and Clustering)
    • 5.1 Document vectorization from lemmatized tokens
    • 5.2 LDA topic modeling with bag-of-words
    • 5.3 TF-IDF clustering experiment (hierarchical + K-means)
    • 5.4 Word2Vec clustering experiment (hierarchical + K-means)
  • 6. Conclusion
  • 7. References

0. Utilities¶

This section centralizes reusable setup.

0.0 Environment setup¶

Run this once per environment to install all required libraries from the project requirements file.

0.1 Imports and package setup¶

Load required Python libraries and NLTK resources used throughout the notebook.

0.2 Global constants and visual theme¶

Define palette values, stopword settings, label mappings, and plotting defaults.

DOMAIN_STOPWORDS contains words that are contextually frequent but not meaningful for semantic analysis in the parliamentary submissions corpus. These include words describing the process or context ("submission", "submissions", "inquiry", "committee", "electoral", "election", "federal", "australian", "australia", "parliament") that do not add value.

PUNCT_SET includes standard punctuation plus additional typographical marks and quotation variants that may occur in parliamentary submissions. This is to robustly detect and remove all forms of punctuation, including ellipses and different quote and dash styles, for thorough token cleaning.

0.3 Data models and metadata parsing classes¶

Define configuration/data classes and metadata parsing logic.

0.4 Corpus ingestion service class¶

Define the service that reads PDFs and builds ingestion records.

0.5 Text preprocessing class and helper functions¶

Define preprocessing utilities, chart helpers, and reusable analysis functions.

1. Introduction¶

This report analyses public submissions to the Australian parliamentary inquiry into the conduct of the 2025 federal election. Parliamentary inquiries play an important role in democratic policy development by collecting evidence from individuals and organisations and translating that evidence into recommendations for parliament. Using Natural Language Processing (NLP), this project transforms a large PDF corpus of submissions into interpretable insights about dominant concerns, recurring themes, and language patterns in public perspectives.

The analysis aims to answer three core questions:

  • What topics and issues appear most frequently across submissions?
  • What are the most frequent inquiries raised by the submissions, the Hansard transcripts, and both combined?
  • Is there any common topic covered by the submissions and the Hansard transcripts?

To address these questions, the workflow combines text extraction, preprocessing, exploratory analysis, and modeling with visual interpretation.

2. Data Ingestion¶

Section Focus¶

This section loads two complementary evidence streams from the Joint Standing Committee on Electoral Matters (JSCEM) inquiry into the 2025 federal election, extracts document text, and builds a unified corpus table for downstream NLP analysis.

Data Sources¶

Evidence stream Folder Files Source
Written evidence (Submissions) docs/submissions/ 299 PDFs JSCEM Submissions page
Oral evidence (Hansard transcripts) docs/hansard_transcripts/ 7 PDFs JSCEM Public Hearings page

Why two sources? Submissions and Hansard transcripts capture different phases of the same parliamentary inquiry. Together they provide a richer and more complete picture of public discourse around electoral reform.

The Relationship: Written vs. Oral Evidence¶

  • Submissions (Written Evidence): A broad "net" cast to the public, allowing any individual, organisation, or government agency to send in a formal document detailing their views, data, or experiences.
  • Public Hearings (Oral Evidence / Hansard): The Committee selects specific witnesses from the pool of people who provided submissions to speak in person. This allows Members of Parliament (MPs) to ask questions, challenge claims, and dig deeper into the most important issues.

High-Level Pipeline: From Inquiry to Law¶

  1. Announcement & Terms of Reference -- Parliament defines exactly what will be investigated (e.g., "The conduct of the 2025 Election").
  2. Call for Submissions -- The public is invited to send in written documents.
  3. Review & Selection -- The Committee staff (Secretariat) reads all submissions and identifies key themes and important witnesses.
  4. Public Hearings -- Selected witnesses testify in person; transcripts (Hansard) are published.
  5. Drafting the Report -- The Committee debates the evidence and writes a final report containing specific Recommendations.
  6. Tabling the Report -- The report is made public in both the House of Representatives and the Senate.
  7. Government Response -- The Prime Minister and Cabinet have approximately six months to formally accept or reject the recommendations.
  8. Legislative Action -- If accepted, the government introduces a Bill to Parliament to officially change the law.

Key Stakeholders and Their Roles¶

Stakeholder Category Role
JSCEM Committee The Investigators A group of MPs and Senators who lead the inquiry, question witnesses, and decide on recommendations.
Committee Secretariat The Engines Non-partisan parliamentary staff who organise hearings, manage submissions, and draft the final report.
The AEC The Experts The Australian Electoral Commission provides technical data on how the election was run and advises if proposed changes are feasible.
Witnesses / Submitters The Evidence Citizens, academic experts, and political parties who provide the raw data and personal stories the inquiry is based on.
The Government The Decision-Makers The Prime Minister and Cabinet, who decide which recommendations will actually become government policy.
The Senate The Gatekeepers The upper house of Parliament that debates and votes on any new laws resulting from the inquiry.

What This Ingestion Step Delivers¶

  • One row per document (submission or Hansard transcript).
  • An evidence_style column (Submission or Hansard) so every later analysis can compare or combine the two evidence streams.
  • Metadata parsed from filenames (submission ID or hearing date, submitter label, submitter type).
  • Extraction diagnostics (page count, extraction status).
  • Text-length features (character and word counts).
  • A single corpus_df dataframe used in all subsequent analysis sections.

3. Exploratory Data Analysis¶

Section Focus¶

This section establishes high level corpus behavior before advanced modeling.

3.1 Corpus overview visuals¶

This subsection provides a high level overview of the corpus dataframe before detailed token analysis. The statistics table and charts summarize corpus composition, document scale, and key structural relationships across submissions.

The main concept here is descriptive statistics: simple measures such as count, mean, median, minimum, maximum, and spread. These do not explain themes yet, but they tell us whether the corpus is balanced, skewed, or dominated by extreme cases.

mean std min 25% 50% 75% max sum
n_pages 8 14 1 2 3 7 120 2,310
char_count 17,566 39,443 52 2,114 5,352 12,640 258,733 5,375,239
word_count_raw 2,757 6,437 8 349 844 1,957 43,400 843,576

The original dataset consisted of 299 submissions and 7 Hansard transcripts (306 documents total). However, 23 submissions were removed because their text could not be reliably extracted from the source PDFs, leaving a working corpus of 276 submissions and 7 Hansard transcripts (283 documents in total).

Document length varies substantially across all three measures (n_pages, char_count, and word_count_raw). This is visible in the large standard deviations and very high maximum values.

A typical document is relatively short (median of 3 pages, 5,803 characters, and 919 words), while the means are noticeably higher (8 pages, 18,979 characters, and 2,979 words). This gap suggests that a smaller number of very long documents pull the averages upward, with extremes reaching 120 pages, 258,733 characters, and 43,400 words.

Overall, the distribution is right-skewed: most documents are short to medium length, and a limited set of long, information dense outliers extend the upper tail.

3.2 Corpus composition by submitter type¶

This chart shows how many submissions come from each inferred submitter category.

The key concept here is metadata based grouping. Instead of looking only at document text, the notebook also groups submissions by information inferred from filenames and entity labels, which helps frame later comparisons across different kinds of submitters.

The corpus is clearly not evenly distributed across submitter types, which means this is not a balanced dataset in the statistical sense. Individual submissions and withheld submissions make up a large share of the corpus, while organisation submissions are fewer in number.

3.3 Distribution of document word counts¶

This histogram shows the spread of document sizes across submissions.

A histogram groups numeric values into bins and shows how often values fall into each range. Here, it helps answer a simple but important question: are most submissions roughly similar in length, or is the corpus dominated by a small number of very short or very long documents?

Most submissions cluster in the shorter to small length range, while a relatively small number extend far to the right of the chart as very large documents. That pattern is called right skew: the majority of values are lower, but a minority of very large cases stretch the distribution upward.

Document with the largest word count (entire row):
file_name submission_id submitter submitter_type evidence_style n_pages extraction_ok raw_text char_count word_count_raw
301 Electoral Matters Joint Committee_2025_11_07.pdf Hansard_2025-11-07 Electoral Matters Joint Committee Hansard Hansard 53 True COMMONWEALTH OF AUSTRALIA \n \nProof Committee... 257242 43400 
Document with the smallest word count (entire row):
file_name submission_id submitter submitter_type evidence_style n_pages extraction_ok raw_text char_count word_count_raw
14 Sub015 - Name withheld.pdf Sub015 Name withheld Withheld Submission 1 True Inquiry into the 2025 federal election\nSubmis... 52 8

Looking directly at the largest and smallest submissions makes the skewness in the histogram concrete. The longest document shows how much detail a single submission can contribute, while the shortest document shows that some cases may function more like brief statements than full reports.

3.4 Relationship between pages and word counts¶

This scatter plot compares page counts with total words to show document scale relationships.

A scatter plot is useful when we want to see whether two numeric variables move together. Here, the question is whether the physical size of a submission in pages aligns with the amount of extracted text in words.

/var/folders/2f/4qv4v6_s6714t7lb2hc6jtqh0000gn/T/ipykernel_94460/1716582564.py:87: UserWarning: 
The palette list has fewer values (3) than needed (5) and will cycle, which may produce an uninterpretable plot.
  sns.scatterplot(data=data, x=x, y=y, hue=hue, palette=palette, alpha=alpha, ax=ax)

The scatter plot reveals a positive but imperfect correlation between page count and word count, confirming that the PDF extraction pipeline has captured text at a scale consistent with the physical size of each document. The relationship is roughly linear for shorter submissions (up to about 20 pages), where each additional page contributes a fairly predictable number of words, but beyond that range the spread increases noticeably, meaning the words per page ratio becomes much less stable in longer documents. Several high page count outliers (above 80 pages) appear in the upper right corner, corresponding to unusually detailed individual submissions that could dominate corpus level statistics if left unweighted, while a handful of moderate page count submissions (around 20 to 35 pages) sit near the y axis baseline, suggesting those PDFs contain substantial non textual content such as images, tables, or scanned pages with limited extractable text. Color coding by submitter_type shows that People (including fictional) submissions span the widest range on both axes, from single page letters to the longest documents in the dataset, indicating highly variable engagement levels, whereas Companies, Agencies, and Institutions tend to cluster in the low to mid range, pointing to more standardized or templated formats.

3.5 Character counts by submitter type¶

This boxplot compares character count distributions by submitter category.

A boxplot summarizes the center, spread, and outliers of a distribution. It is especially useful when document lengths vary widely, because it shows the median and the typical range more clearly than a simple bar chart.

/var/folders/2f/4qv4v6_s6714t7lb2hc6jtqh0000gn/T/ipykernel_94460/1716582564.py:68: FutureWarning: 

Passing `palette` without assigning `hue` is deprecated and will be removed in v0.14.0. Assign the `x` variable to `hue` and set `legend=False` for the same effect.

  sns.boxplot(data=data, x=x, y=y, palette=palette, ax=ax)

Character counts vary substantially both within and across submitter types. Individual submissions (People) have a low median but the widest spread, with extreme outliers exceeding 200,000 characters, while organizational submissions (Companies, Agencies, Institutions) cluster around a higher median with a broader interquartile range, reflecting more consistently substantial report documents. Withheld submissions form the most compact distribution with the lowest median, and the Unknown category, despite few data points, shows a surprisingly high median and wide range. Hansard transcripts stand out as the longest documents by a wide margin: their median exceeds 200,000 characters and even their lower quartile sits above the maximum of every other group, which is expected given that each transcript captures an entire parliamentary hearing session rather than a single written submission. The numerous outliers above the upper whiskers across the non Hansard groups confirm the heavy right skew seen in earlier charts, meaning a small number of very long documents carry a disproportionate share of the total text. The stark length gap between Hansard and all other categories also justifies treating these transcripts separately in downstream analyses (e.g., per document normalization) so that their volume does not dominate corpus wide statistics. This supports the later use of median based summaries, per document normalization, and cleaned token counts rather than relying solely on corpus wide totals.

3.6 Linguistic annotation sample (NER and POS)¶

This section uses two common NLP annotation tasks to make the language in the corpus more interpretable before preprocessing and modeling.

Named Entity Recognition (NER) identifies spans of text corresponding to real world entities such as people, organisations, places, dates, laws, or quantities. In a corpus like this one, NER is helpful because it shows which institutions, actors, and locations are explicitly named in the submissions.

Part of Speech (POS) tagging assigns a grammatical role to each token, such as noun, verb, adjective, or determiner. It does not tell us the topic directly, but it helps us understand what kind of language dominates the corpus and whether the text is likely to support later thematic analysis.

Named Entity Recognition (NER) sample view¶

The rendered sample below is included as a qualitative view rather than a full quantitative analysis. Its purpose is to make entity detection visible to the reader so the report does not treat NER as a hidden black box.

Sample sentences with detected named entities:
Time to revisit territory representation Introduction The ACT has had the same Senate ORG representation with the same anomalous election arrangements for 50 years DATE !
During that time the ACT electoral enrolment has increased by 143% PERCENT and the size of the State ORG representation in the Senate ORG has increased by 20% PERCENT but the ACT representation has remained unchanged.
The Territories also continue to be disadvantaged by shorter terms and the simultaneous elections provisions which were included in the 1974 DATE legislation to establish territory representation.
Submission In 1975 DATE the enrolment for the first ORDINAL ACT Senate ORG election was 115916 DATE .
The 1984 DATE changes increased the discrepancy between state and territory representation.
State Senate ORG representation increased by 20% PERCENT but Territory representation remained the same.
Given that the constitutional design of Senate ORG representation is based on enhanced representation of smaller jurisdictions it is appropriate to compare trends in the ACT with those of the least populated states.
The population growth in the ACT is significantly greater than the increase in the two CARDINAL smallest states, Tasmania GPE and South Australia GPE .
In the ACT the increase in electoral enrolment has been 143% PERCENT since 1975 DATE compared to 61% PERCENT in Tasmania GPE and 65% PERCENT in South Australia GPE .
Enrolment in the ACT has grown from 45% PERCENT of that in Tasmania GPE in 1975 DATE to 78% PERCENT in 2025 DATE .
 
Entity labels detected in the rendered sample:
entity_label count
0 DATE 8
1 PERCENT 8
2 ORG 6
3 GPE 5
4 ORDINAL 1
5 CARDINAL 1

The rendered sample confirms that spaCy reliably detects the entity types central to electoral policy text: ORG for institutions like the Senate, GPE for jurisdictions such as Tasmania and South Australia, DATE for legislative time references, and PERCENT / CARDINAL for the quantitative evidence submitters use to support their arguments. The high density of annotations in just a few sentences shows that the corpus is rich in formal, fact laden language where named entities carry much of the argumentative weight. NER is not perfect, as some labels may be wrong or entities missed, but this qualitative view makes the detection step transparent so that later results built on entity features can be interpreted with appropriate confidence.

Part of Speech (POS) analysis¶

The chart below tags words in a runtime capped sample of submission text and summarizes the most frequent grammatical categories.

pos_tag pos_description count
11 VBD Verb, past tense 5193
10 VBG Verb, gerund/present participle 5305
9 VBN Verb, past participle 5626
8 TO to 6401
7 RB Adverb 7208
6 CC Coordinating conjunction 8055
5 VB Verb, base form 8228
4 NNS Noun, plural 18568
3 DT Determiner 22413
2 JJ Adjective 22473
1 IN Preposition/subordinating conjunction 27606
0 NN Noun, singular/common 46556

Nouns, prepositions, determiners, and adjectives dominate the POS profile. That is typical of formal policy style submissions because these documents spend a lot of time naming institutions, actors, procedures, and issues rather than using conversational or narrative language.

4. Preprocessing¶

4.1 Prepare cleaned tokens and lexical metrics¶

This cell converts the raw text into analysis ready form through a sequence of steps, each designed to progressively reduce the dimensionality of the data so that later models work with a smaller, more meaningful vocabulary instead of a noisy, inflated one.

Step 1 — URL cleanup and clean tokens. Before tokenization, URLs are removed from raw_text using a regex pattern (matching both http(s):// and www. links) so web addresses do not pollute lexical features. The clean_tokens method then applies POS filtering to keep only nouns, proper nouns, adjectives, and verbs, which are the parts of speech that carry the most topical meaning. Stopwords, punctuation, and non alphabetic tokens are discarded. The result is a focused token list that removes high frequency, low information words and dramatically reduces the number of unique features the corpus contains.

Step 2 — Bigram phrase detection. Gensim's Phrases model is used with scoring="npmi" to detect collocations in the cleaned token stream. Bigrams that satisfy min_count=3 and threshold=0.5 are merged into underscore phrases (for example, electoral + commission -> electoral_commission) so the representation keeps multi word concepts as single units while filtering weaker pairs.

Step 3 — Trigram extraction. Trigrams are generated directly from cleaned tokens using nltk.trigrams, producing contiguous three word windows that are stored as underscore joined phrase tokens.

Step 4 — Lemmatization. Each token is mapped to its base dictionary form using NLTK's WordNetLemmatizer. Penn Treebank POS tags are converted to WordNet tags so the lemmatizer can apply the correct morphological rules (for example, votes, voted, and voting all become vote, while adjectives and adverbs are handled by their own rules). By merging inflected variants into a common lemma, this step compresses the vocabulary even further, ensuring that surface level variation does not artificially inflate feature counts in downstream models like TF-IDF, topic modeling, and clustering.

Finally, the cell computes per document metrics including cleaned word count and lexical diversity (the ratio of unique tokens to total tokens) so these values are available for all subsequent comparisons.

file_name clean_tokens pmi_bigram_tokens pmi_trigram_tokens combined_phrase_tokens lemmatized_tokens
0 Sub001 - Bob McMullan.pdf [time, revisit, territory, representation, int... [time, revisit, territory, representation, int... [time_revisit_territory, revisit_territory_rep... [time, revisit, territory, representation, int... [time, revisit, territory, representation, int...
1 Sub002 - Dr Sheppard.pdf [national, university, possible, solutions, mo... [national, university, possible, solutions, mo... [national_university_possible, university_poss... [national, university, possible, solutions, mo... [national, university, possible, solution, mon...
2 Sub003 - Grattan Institute.pdf [grattan, institute, september, aruna, kate, g... [grattan_institute, september, aruna, kate, gr... [grattan_institute_september, institute_septem... [grattan_institute, september, aruna, kate, gr... [grattan_institute, september, aruna, kate, gr...
3 Sub004 - Australian Electoral Commission.pdf [aec, joint, standing, matters, aec, joint, st... [aec, joint_standing, matters, aec, joint_stan... [aec_joint_standing, joint_standing_matters, s... [aec, joint_standing, matters, joint_standing,... [aec, joint_standing, matter, joint_standing, ...
4 Sub005 - The Australia Institute.pdf [democracy, thriving, inventive, democracy, me... [democracy, thriving, inventive, democracy, me... [democracy_thriving_inventive, thriving_invent... [democracy, thriving, inventive, measured, ref... [democracy, thrive, inventive, measure, reform...
5 Sub006 - Andrew Murray.pdf [secretary, joint, standing, matters, box, hou... [secretary_joint, standing_matters, box, house... [secretary_joint_standing, joint_standing_matt... [secretary_joint, standing_matters, box, house... [secretary_joint, standing_matters, box, house...
6 Sub007 - Genevieve Grant.pdf [page, written, joint, standing, matters, refe... [page, written, joint_standing, matters, refer... [page_written_joint, written_joint_standing, j... [page, written, joint_standing, matters, refer... [page, write, joint_standing, matter, referenc...
7 Sub008 - Richard Ure.pdf [years, epping, first, suburb, sydney, declare... [years, epping, first, suburb, sydney, declare... [years_epping_first, epping_first_suburb, firs... [years, epping, first, suburb, sydney, declare... [year, epping, first, suburb, sydney, declare,...
8 Sub009 - Barry Searle.pdf [barry, searle, sept, time, banned, poll, boot... [barry, searle, sept, time, banned, poll, boot... [barry_searle_sept, searle_sept_time, sept_tim... [barry, searle, sept, time, banned, poll, boot... [barry, searle, sept, time, ban, poll, booth, ...
9 Sub010 - Name withheld.pdf [regarding, brief, voted, day, polling, ended,... [regarding, brief, voted, day, polling, ended,... [regarding_brief_voted, brief_voted_day, voted... [regarding, brief, voted, day, polling, ended,... [regard, brief, vote, day, polling, end, wait,...

This preprocessing step creates several parallel versions of the text, from raw tokens to cleaned tokens, phrase-aware tokens, and finally lemmatized tokens. That layered approach is useful because different tasks need different text representations: simple counts can use clean_tokens, phrase analysis needs the bigram output, and modeling benefits from the more normalized preprocessed_text field.

file_name submission_id submitter submitter_type evidence_style n_pages extraction_ok raw_text char_count word_count_raw ... combined_phrase_tokens lemmatized_tokens preprocessed_text clean_word_count lexical_diversity dominant_topic tfidf_hierarchical_cluster tfidf_kmeans_cluster w2v_hierarchical_cluster w2v_kmeans_cluster

283 rows × 25 columns

4.2 Most common words¶

This subsection focuses on visualization only. Using the cleaned tokens prepared in the previous step, it shows the most common terms across the full corpus and within each submitter_type.

The concept here is term frequency: counting how often words appear. Frequency is simple, but it is often the clearest first signal of what the corpus is about once obvious stopwords and formatting noise have been removed.

Because these charts use clean_tokens, the dominant terms are easier to interpret than raw token counts and better reflect substantive inquiry themes rather than formatting noise or stopwords.

Overall corpus. The corpus chart is dominated by aec (~2,600 occurrences), confirming the Australian Electoral Commission as the institutional anchor of the inquiry. A cluster of democratic process terms political, people, party, voters, vote, polling, voting — forms the next tier (roughly 2,000–2,500 each), reflecting the core subject matter: elections, political participation, and polling logistics. Lower ranked but still prominent terms like volunteers, candidates, senate, elections, public, and campaign fill out the operational vocabulary of electoral administration.

Hansard transcripts. The high-frequency filler think (witnesses saying "I think…") was excluded as a Hansard-specific stopword because it reflects oral register rather than substantive content. With that removed, people stands out as the clear leader (~1,300), nearly double the next token, confirming that oral testimony centres on public interest and everyday citizens. chair (~800) follows, reflecting the procedural turn-taking of committee hearings ("Chair, I would like to…"). A mid-tier cluster — aec (~700), polling (~680), joint (~650), party and terms (~580), senator (~550) — captures the institutional and procedural vocabulary unique to parliamentary discourse. Lower-ranked terms like volunteers, time, voters, booth, vote, know, and going introduce an operational and conversational layer that is absent from written submissions, reinforcing that Hansard transcripts blend electoral substance with the informal, deliberative register of live testimony.

Faceted charts by submitter type. The per-group breakdown reveals how each stakeholder frames the inquiry differently:

  • Companies, Agencies, Institutions, etc. — aec leads by a wide margin (~680), followed by political and party. This group speaks in institutional and regulatory language, foregrounding agencies, policy, and elections administration.
  • Hansard — with think excluded, people and chair lead, followed by aec, polling, and joint, reflecting the oral, deliberative nature of transcribed hearings and their procedural vocabulary.
  • People, including fictional — vote is the clear leader (~1,150), followed by senate, party, and voters. Individual submitters centre their language on the act of voting itself and the senate electoral system, suggesting grassroots concerns about ballot access and representation.
  • Unknown — political leads (~265), with voters and aec also prominent. The vocabulary resembles the institutional group but at lower scale, likely representing submissions where the submitter category could not be determined but the content still tracks mainstream inquiry themes.
  • Withheld — volunteers is the top token (~475), a unique emphasis not seen in any other group. polling, voters, and candidate follow. This pattern suggests that withheld submitters are disproportionately concerned with the volunteer workforce at polling places and candidate level issues, possibly reflecting electoral experience from individuals who preferred anonymity.

4.3 Corpus snapshot statistics¶

This cell reports compact EDA summary statistics used to frame the visual analysis.

Think of this as a checkpoint in the story: after cleaning the corpus, the notebook pauses to summarize how large, varied, and information rich the text now looks before moving into n grams and models.

=== EDA Snapshot ===
Documents analysed: 283
Corpus vocabulary size (clean tokens): 17,304
Median document word count (raw): 919
Median lexical diversity: 0.644

4.4 Frequency table preparation (unigrams and NPMI bigrams)¶

This cell computes unigram frequencies from the cleaned token stream and phrase frequencies from the NPMI bigram output so the following charts can compare raw n grams against detected phrase units.

What these concepts mean: A unigram is a single token. A bigram is a two word sequence. NPMI (Normalized Pointwise Mutual Information) is a collocation measure that helps identify word combinations that occur together more meaningfully than chance would suggest.

This matters because raw adjacency alone can produce many common but uninformative phrases, whereas NPMI helps highlight combinations that behave more like stable concepts.

Top 10 raw unigrams:
term count
0 the 48294
1 to 24489
2 of 24459
3 and 21129
4 a 14984
5 in 14823
6 that 13787
7 i 9125
8 for 8491
9 is 8052 
Top 10 clean unigrams:
term count
0 aec 2569
1 political 2445
2 people 2430
3 party 2393
4 voters 2349
5 vote 2289
6 polling 2181
7 voting 2075
8 number 1725
9 think 1652 
Top 10 raw bigrams:
bigram count
0 of the 5897
1 in the 3561
2 to the 2804
3 federal election 2802
4 the federal 2648
5 into the 2387
6 inquiry into 2231
7 election submission 1963
8 the aec 1914
9 for the 1809 
Top 10 phrase bigrams:
bigram count
0 house representatives 588
1 pre poll 477
2 polling booth 435
3 political parties 430
4 polling booths 419
5 joint standing 318
6 matters joint 306
7 joint page 304
8 third party 301
9 polling places 299

trigram count

1 inquiry into the 2191

trigram count

9 state division ppvc 81

4.5 Top unigram frequency chart¶

These charts visualize the most common single word terms in separate raw token and cleaned token views so the effect of preprocessing is easy to compare.

A unigram frequency chart is one of the simplest NLP views, but it is very useful for storytelling because it shows how the vocabulary changes once cleaning decisions have been applied.

Showing raw and clean unigram charts separately makes the effect of preprocessing much easier to see. The raw token view still contains more generic language, while the cleaned token view concentrates attention on terms that are easier to connect to substantive inquiry themes.

What the result suggests. Terms such as political, voters, aec, and vote indicate that the corpus remains strongly centered on electoral administration, participation, and institutional process even after general context words are filtered out. That is a useful sign that the cleaning strategy is preserving topic relevant vocabulary rather than stripping too much meaning away.

4.6 Bigram phrase visuals (frequency chart and network graph)¶

This subsection compares frequent raw bigrams with detected NPMI bigram phrases and then visualizes the phrase based bigram structure as a network using the lemmatized phrase tokens.

The two main ideas here are collocations and networks. A collocation is a word combination that tends to appear together, while a network graph helps show how those phrase elements connect across the corpus rather than only how often they appear.

Bigrams add more context than single terms, and the NPMI phrase model highlights word pairs that cohere strongly enough to be treated as phrase like units. Comparing raw bigrams with detected NPMI bigram phrases shows the difference between simple adjacency and more meaningful collocations.

4.7 Top three-gram frequency chart¶

This section compares raw trigram frequencies with phrase-detected trigrams to show which three-word patterns emerge directly from token sequences versus phrase model detection.

The trigram visual now complements the bigram analysis by highlighting higher-order phrase patterns for interpretation.

4.8 Word cloud of preprocessed corpus¶

This visual summary highlights the relative prominence of terms in the fully preprocessed corpus.

A word cloud is a communication oriented visualization: larger words appear more prominent, but the chart is approximate rather than exact. It works well as a storytelling device at the end of the preprocessing section because it gives a quick visual impression of the vocabulary before the notebook moves into formal modeling.

The largest terms like polling_place, polling_booth, pre_poll, ballot_paper, house, representatives, fixed_term are all NPMI detected bigram phrases, confirming that the phrase detection step successfully elevated multi word concepts above their individual components. The physical infrastructure of elections (polling_place, polling_booth, pre_poll) visually dominates, suggesting that the corpus is heavily concerned with where and how Australians cast their votes. Institutional phrases like house (of) representatives, joint_standing, matters_joint, and joint_page reflect the parliamentary committee context in which the inquiry operates.

Single token terms fill the surrounding space: vote, candidate, voter, party, political, make, number, time, volunteer, and campaign. These map neatly onto the democratic process vocabulary already seen in the bar charts but here appear alongside their phrasal counterparts, reinforcing that electoral participation and candidate selection are the twin pillars of the discourse. Smaller but still visible terms like social_media, third_party, political_advertising, truth_political_advertising, postal_vote, vote_card point to secondary policy debates around campaign regulation, media influence, and alternative voting methods.

5. Advanced Methods (Topic Modeling and Clustering)¶

This section extends the descriptive analysis with topic modeling and unsupervised clustering so the corpus can be compared through latent themes, document groupings, and broader patterns of policy discussion.

Core concepts introduced here:

  • LDA topic modeling estimates hidden themes by finding groups of words that tend to appear together across documents.
  • TF IDF gives more weight to terms that help distinguish one document from another.
  • Clustering groups documents that are similar in vocabulary or semantic profile.
  • Dimensionality reduction simplifies high dimensional text features so they can be modeled and visualized more clearly.
  • PCA (Principal Component Analysis) is a linear dimensionality reduction technique that projects the data onto orthogonal axes of maximum variance, useful as an initial reduction step before clustering or visualization.

5.1 Document Vectorization from Lemmatized Tokens¶

This section builds three separate document-level feature representations from corpus_df["lemmatized_tokens"]:

  1. Bag of Words counts (X_bow_counts)
  2. TF-IDF (X_tfidf)
  3. Continuous Bag of Words Word2Vec document embeddings (X_cbow_w2v)
Documents available for vectorization: 283
Example document text: time revisit territory representation introduction act senate anomalous arrangement year enrolment increase size state remain unchanged terr...

BoW matrix shape: (283, 8552)
BoW vocabulary size: 8552

TF-IDF matrix shape: (283, 8552)
TF-IDF vocabulary size: 8552

Exception ignored in: 'gensim.models.word2vec_inner.our_dot_float'

CBOW Word2Vec matrix shape: (283, 100)
CBOW embedding dimension: 100
CBOW vocabulary size: 13339

5.2 LDA Topic Modeling with Bag-of-Words¶

This cell evaluates candidate topic counts with a coherence score, selects the strongest LDA solution, and summarizes each topic using high probability terms.

What is LDA? Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) is a topic modeling method that treats each document as a mixture of topics and each topic as a mixture of words. It does not assign one fixed label to a document by itself, instead, it estimates probabilities that indicate how strongly each topic appears in each submission.

What is coherence? Topic coherence is a quality check that estimates whether the top words in a topic make semantic sense together. In this notebook, coherence is used to compare several candidate topic counts so the final model is selected on an interpretable basis rather than by guesswork.

k coherence_score
0 2 0.699779
1 3 0.643376
2 4 0.629467
3 5 0.623522
4 6 0.624169
5 7 0.595756
6 8 0.506835
7 9 0.558142
8 10 0.547813
9 11 0.564283
10 12 0.546716
11 13 0.538728
12 14 0.536083
13 15 0.529180
topic top_terms
0 Topic 1 house_representatives, polling_booth, matters_...
1 Topic 2 pre_poll, polling_place, polling_booth, vote, ...

Coherence curve. The coherence plot peaks at k = 2 (c_v ≈ 0.70), then declines steadily.

Topic distribution. The dominant-topic bar chart reveals a heavily imbalanced split: Topic 2 absorbs the vast majority of submissions (~260), while Topic 1 captures only ~20 documents.

Topic 1: Parliamentary procedure and institutional structure. The top terms are house_representatives, polling_booth, matters_joint, page_matters_joint, joint_page_matters, joint_page, ballot_paper, senator_colbeck, year_terms, and political_party. These are heavily procedural and institutional: references to the House of Representatives, joint committee page metadata, specific senators, and fixed parliamentary terms.

Topic 2: Electoral operations and voter participation. The top terms are pre_poll, polling_place, polling_booth, vote, make, candidate, voter, party, volunteer, and include. This is the dominant topic and it reads as a coherent policy discussion about the practical conduct of elections.

5.3 TF-IDF Clustering Experiment (Hierarchical + K-means)¶

This section applies hierarchical clustering and K-means on TF-IDF features to identify document groupings and compare cluster structure across methods.

Cluster selection rationale. The silhouette curve peaks at k = 2 (≈ 0.22), indicating that a coarse two way split maximises average intra cluster cohesion. However, a two cluster solution is too coarse for thematic interpretation of a parliamentary inquiry corpus. As k increases the silhouette score declines and flattens around k = 10–16, reflecting the natural overlap among submissions that share common electoral vocabulary. We select k = 16 to preserve fine grained topical distinctions at this granularity the algorithm separates clearly differentiated policy sub-themes even though overall silhouette (≈ 0.08) is modest. Low silhouette is expected with high-dimensional sparse TF-IDF vectors and Ward linkage on SVD-reduced features.

Dendrogram structure. The truncated dendrogram reveals an asymmetric merge tree. Two major branches emerge at the highest distance level (≈ 2.8): one groups the larger, more general electoral clusters (left branch), while the other captures specialised and smaller thematic clusters (right branch). Several early low distance merges on the right side correspond to tightly focused micro topics (e.g., voting system reform proposals, audit procedures), whereas the left branch merges occur at higher distances, reflecting greater internal heterogeneity in broad electoral clusters.

Thematic interpretation of selected clusters.

  • Cluster 3 (26 docs) — Political advertising and truth in political advertising. Top terms include truth_political_advertising, social_media, accessed_october, and third_party.
  • Cluster 4 (12 docs) — Pre-poll voting operations. Dominated by pre_poll, pre_poll_voting, brighton_pre_poll, and htv_cards.
  • Cluster 10 (16 docs) — Fixed terms and proportional representation. Terms such as house_representatives, fixed_terms, proportional_representation, section_constitution, and prime_minister.
  • Cluster 11 (8 docs) — Proxy voting and community-specific concerns. The distinctive terms jewish_community, proxy_groups, and third_party_proxy.
  • Cluster 12 (3 docs) — Alternative ranking systems. linear_ranking_system, preferential_linear_ranking, and hare_clark.
  • Cluster 15 (4 docs) — Political funding and expenditure caps. public_funding, donation_caps, expenditure_caps, and nominated_entity

Source-type breakdown. Almost all clusters draw exclusively from written submissions, with zero Hansard transcripts. The notable exception is Cluster 8 (7 docs), which is composed entirely of Hansard records (page_matters_joint, senator_colbeck, senator_mulholland, chair_thank), capturing parliamentary committee hearing discourse rather than public submissions.

Limitations of this partition. The low overall silhouette score reflects substantial vocabulary overlap, many submissions discuss polling, volunteers, and voting in similar terms regardless of their primary policy focus. Clusters 1 and 6 act as residual "catch-all" groups, absorbing documents that do not contain sufficiently distinctive n-grams.

The elbow plot shows a clear bend around k = 4–5, after which inertia decreases more gradually, supporting k = 5 as a reasonable choice from a diminishing returns perspective. The silhouette plot, however, reveals that k = 5 (≈ 0.075) sits near a local trough, while the auto best k = 10 reaches ≈ 0.095. Selecting k = 5 therefore prioritises interpretability fewer, broader clusters that are easier to label thematically over maximum statistical separation.

PCA scatter plot. The two-component PCA projection shows substantial overlap among the five clusters, with most documents concentrated in a central cloud between PC1 ∈ [−0.1, 0.2] and PC2 ∈ [−0.1, 0.2]. No clean spatial boundaries are visible, confirming that the clusters represent soft thematic tendencies rather than hard-edged separations. The clearest outliers extend along PC1 for Clusters 4 and 5, suggesting these groups contain documents with more distinctive term profiles.

Cluster size distribution. The partition is strongly imbalanced: Clusters 1 (110) and 4 (100) together absorb 74 % of the corpus and act as broad catc all groups, while Clusters 2 (24), 3 (12), and 5 (37) capture more focused thematic niches.

Thematic interpretation of each cluster.

  • Cluster 1 (110 docs) — Polling operations and volunteer experience. Top TF-IDF terms: polling_booth, volunteer, candidate, vote, booth, voter, polling_place, make, experience, hand. The vocabulary centres on people who worked at or attended polling places, with volunteer, experience, and hand signalling first hand, grassroots accounts.

  • Cluster 2 (24 docs) — AEC conduct and polling place incidents. Top terms: polling_place, pre_poll, polling_booth, term_reference, aec_officials, terms_reference, early_voting, obstruction_intimidating, deliberate_obstruction_intimidating, behaviour_voters_political.

  • Cluster 3 (12 docs) — Parliamentary proceedings and voting-system reform. Top terms: joint_page_matters, page_matters_joint, joint_page, matters_joint, senator_colbeck, november_joint_page, preferential_linear_ranking, linear_ranking_system, engaged_influencers, senator_mulholland. Two sub themes converge: (a) Hansard committee metadata (joint_page_matters, senator_colbeck, senator_mulholland) and (b) alternative ranking system proposals (preferential_linear_ranking, linear_ranking_system).

  • Cluster 4 (100 docs) — Policy reform and institutional electoral framework. Top terms: house_representatives, political_party, year_terms, increase, require, ballot_paper, ballot_papers, polling_place, make, change. Unlike Cluster 1's experiential register, Cluster 4 centres on institutional structures (house_representatives, political_party).

  • Cluster 5 (37 docs) — Pre-poll voting and ACT-specific concerns. Top terms: pre_poll, volunteer, house_canberra_act, canberra_act, box_house_canberra, matters_box_house, standing_matters_box, joint_standing_matters, standing_matters, vote.

5.4 Word2Vec Clustering Experiment (Hierarchical + K-means)¶

This section clusters CBOW Word2Vec document embeddings with hierarchical clustering and K-means, then interprets clusters using token back-mapping.

What these concepts mean: Word2Vec embeddings represent semantic context in dense vectors rather than sparse term counts. K-means assigns documents to fixed cluster centroids, while hierarchical clustering reveals nested similarity structure.

This complements TF-IDF by testing whether semantic embeddings produce coherent clusters beyond frequency-based weighting.

Word2Vec hierarchical clusters (selected=5, auto-best by silhouette=5):
- Cluster 1 (82 docs)
  polling_booth, pre_poll, vote, candidate, voter, volunteer, polling_place, make, party, campaign, need, time
- Cluster 2 (15 docs)
  house_representatives, ballot_paper, political_party, per_cent, joint_standing_matters, vote_value, joint_standing, public_funding, first_preference, new_south, new_south_wales, name_name_name
- Cluster 3 (110 docs)
  polling_booth, pre_poll, vote, volunteer, candidate, make, voter, party, campaign, time, polling_place, include
- Cluster 4 (1 docs)
  vulnerablevoters, framesandparty, candidate, withoutvoting, prepollbooths, vulnerablevoters_framesandparty_candidate, framesandparty_candidate_withoutvoting, candidate_withoutvoting_prepollbooths
- Cluster 5 (75 docs)
  polling_booth, polling_place, matters_joint, joint_page, joint_page_matters, page_matters_joint, house_representatives, political_party, pre_poll, third_party, senator_colbeck, social_media

The silhouette curve peaks at num of clusters = 5 (≈ 0.54). Notably, the Word2Vec silhouette scores are substantially higher than those observed in the TF-IDF experiments (≈ 0.08–0.22), confirming that dense CBOW embeddings produce tighter, better separated document clusters than sparse frequency based representations. Beyond num of clusters = 6 the curve drops sharply to ≈ 0.43 and remains flat through k = 20.

  • Cluster 1 (82 docs) — Polling operations and voter participation. Top terms: polling_booth, pre_poll, vote, candidate, voter, volunteer, polling_place, make, party, campaign, need, time.

  • Cluster 2 (15 docs) — Institutional electoral framework and funding. Top terms: house_representatives, ballot_paper, political_party, per_cent, joint_standing_matters, vote_value, joint_standing, public_funding, first_preference, new_south, new_south_wales, name_name_name. All 15 documents are written submissions. The vocabulary is distinctly institutional house_representatives, political_party, public_funding, vote_value indicating formal policy proposals around parliamentary representation, funding mechanisms, and preferential voting. The term new_south_wales suggests a geographic focus on NSW electoral matters.

  • Cluster 3 (110 docs) — General election discourse. Top terms: polling_booth, pre_poll, vote, volunteer, candidate, make, voter, party, campaign, time, polling_place, include. The largest cluster overlaps heavily with Cluster 1 in its top vocabulary, differing mainly in rank order (volunteer rises and need is replaced by include).

  • Cluster 4 (1 doc) — Outlier with corrupted tokens. Top terms: vulnerablevoters, framesandparty, candidate, withoutvoting, prepollbooths, vulnerablevoters_framesandparty_candidate, framesandparty_candidate_withoutvoting, candidate_withoutvoting_prepollbooths. This singleton contains concatenated, malformed tokens (e.g., vulnerablevoters, framesandparty, withoutvoting).

  • Cluster 5 (75 docs) — Parliamentary proceedings and mixed policy themes. Top terms: polling_booth, polling_place, matters_joint, joint_page, joint_page_matters, page_matters_joint, house_representatives, political_party, pre_poll, third_party, senator_colbeck, social_media. This is the only cluster containing Hansard transcripts (7 out of 75 documents).

Word2Vec K-means selected k: 4 (auto-best by silhouette was 4)

Word2Vec K-means clusters (back-mapped lemmatized tokens):
- Cluster 1 (150 docs)
  polling_booth, polling_place, pre_poll, house_representatives, matters_joint, joint_page, joint_page_matters, page_matters_joint, vote, make, political_party, third_party
- Cluster 2 (25 docs)
  house_representatives, ballot_paper, ppvc_nsw, political_party, social_media, per_cent, vote_value, joint_standing_matters, joint_standing, state_division, ppvc_total, ppvc_total_votes
- Cluster 3 (107 docs)
  polling_booth, candidate, vote, pre_poll, volunteer, voter, polling_place, make, party, campaign, time, need
- Cluster 4 (1 docs)
  vulnerablevoters, framesandparty, candidate, withoutvoting, prepollbooths, vulnerablevoters_framesandparty_candidate, framesandparty_candidate_withoutvoting, candidate_withoutvoting_prepollbooths

The elbow plot shows a pronounced bend at k = 4: inertia falls steeply from ≈ 36 (k = 2) to ≈ 15 (k = 4), then flattens into a gentle decline through k = 10, confirming that the fourth centroid captures the last substantial chunk of within cluster variance. The silhouette plot reinforces this choice the silhouette score peaks at k = 4 (≈ 0.52), the highest across all candidates.

Comparison with hierarchical clustering. The hierarchical experiment used num of clusters = 5 and achieved a peak silhouette of ≈ 0.54. K-means at k = 4 reaches ≈ 0.52, a marginally lower score but with one fewer cluster, resulting in a simpler partition that sacrifices very little cohesion. The main structural difference is that the hierarchical five cluster solution splits the large polling/proceedings group into two sub clusters, whereas K-means folds them into a single large Cluster 1 (150 docs).

  • Cluster 1 (150 docs) Polling logistics and parliamentary proceedings. Top terms: polling_booth, polling_place, pre_poll, house_representatives, matters_joint, joint_page, vote, make, political_party, third_party. This is the largest cluster and the only one containing Hansard transcripts (6 of 150 docs). The vocabulary blends two registers: written submissions focused on physical polling infrastructure (polling_booth, polling_place, pre_poll) and parliamentary committee language (matters_joint, joint_page, house_representatives). This merging suggests that, in the Word2Vec embedding space, the procedural language of Hansard is semantically closer to polling logistics submissions than to institutional policy text.

  • Cluster 2 (25 docs) Institutional policy, electoral statistics, and media regulation. Top terms: house_representatives, ballot_paper, ppvc_nsw, political_party, social_media, per_cent, vote_value, joint_standing_matters, state_division, ppvc_total_votes. Almost exclusively written submissions (24 of 25), this cluster stands out for its quantitative and regulatory vocabulary — per_cent, vote_value, ppvc_total, state_division — pointing to documents that discuss electoral formulas, proportional representation, and state level vote distributions. The presence of social_media and ppvc_nsw further indicates submissions addressing media regulation and NSW specific Pre Poll Voting Centre data. The single Hansard document in this cluster uses committee calendar tokens (friday, october), suggesting it was pulled in by scheduling metadata rather than policy content.

  • Cluster 3 (107 docs) Grassroots election participation and campaigning. Top terms: polling_booth, candidate, vote, pre_poll, volunteer, voter, polling_place, make, party, campaign, time, need. Entirely composed of written submissions (107 of 107), with zero Hansard representation. The distinguishing terms relative to Cluster 1 are volunteer, candidate, campaign, time, and need — action oriented, grassroots vocabulary that foregrounds the human experience of volunteering at booths, running as a candidate, and managing campaign logistics.

  • Cluster 4 (1 doc) Outlier with concatenated tokens. Top terms: vulnerablevoters, framesandparty, withoutvoting, prepollbooths. A singleton submission whose text was extracted without proper whitespace, producing fused multi word tokens. The malformed vocabulary has no overlap with any other cluster, causing K-means to isolate it as an extreme outlier.

6. Conclusion¶

Answering the research questions¶

1. What topics and issues appear most frequently across submissions?

The corpus is anchored by a consistent core vocabulary centred on the Australian Electoral Commission (aec), democratic participation (vote, voters, voting, party, political, people), and the physical infrastructure of elections (polling_place, polling_booth, pre_poll, ballot_paper). NPMI phrase detection elevated multi word policy concepts — fixed_term, political_advertising, truth_political_advertising, social_media, postal_vote, house representatives, joint_standing — that single token frequency charts would have missed. LDA topic modeling confirmed these thematic clusters at a latent level, grouping terms into coherent topics around electoral administration, parliamentary procedure, campaign regulation, and voter access. Together, the evidence shows that the inquiry discourse is dominated by the practical mechanics of how elections are run, with secondary but persistent attention to advertising regulation and media influence.

2. What are the most frequent inquiries raised by the submissions and the Hansard transcripts?

Written submissions are dominated by the practical mechanics of elections: polling_booth, polling_place, pre_poll, vote, candidate, and volunteer rank highest, revealing that the public's primary concerns centre on polling logistics, ballot access, and the volunteer workforce at voting centres. Hansard transcripts foreground procedural and deliberative language people, chair, senator, joint_standing, committee reflecting the structured question and answer format of live parliamentary hearings.

3. Is there any common topic covered by the submissions and the Hansard transcripts?

Yes. Despite their different registers formal written submissions versus conversational oral testimony — both evidence streams share a broad thematic core around the AEC, polling logistics, political parties, and voter participation. Terms like aec, polling, party, voters, and political appear prominently in both the overall corpus chart and the Hansard specific chart. The word cloud of the fully preprocessed corpus confirms this overlap visually, with polling_place, polling_booth, ballot_paper, and vote dominating regardless of evidence style. Where the two streams diverge is in register and emphasis: Hansard transcripts carry more procedural vocabulary (chair, senator, joint, terms) and conversational markers (know, going, time), while written submissions are denser in policy specific phrases (fixed_term, political_advertising, postal_vote). The shared core confirms that both streams address the same inquiry, while the divergences reflect the complementary roles of written evidence and oral examination in parliamentary processes.

Practical implications¶

  • For the JSCEM and policymakers: The NLP analysis surfaces the dominant public concerns without requiring manual reading of all 299 submissions. Polling place logistics, volunteer management, ballot paper design, and political advertising regulation emerge as the issues with the broadest constituency across submitter types.
  • For future inquiry analysis: The modular pipeline (ingestion, preprocessing, phrase detection, modeling) is reusable for other parliamentary inquiries. The Hansard specific stopword approach demonstrates that evidence stream aware preprocessing can improve interpretability without distorting the underlying data.

Limitations¶

  • Corpus scale. With 306 documents the corpus is modest; larger inquiries would benefit from the same pipeline but may require more aggressive dimensionality reduction or sampling strategies.
  • PDF extraction noise. Some submissions contained scanned images, headers/footers, and formatting artifacts that survived text extraction. While preprocessing removed much of this noise, a small residual effect on token counts is likely.
  • Submitter type inference. The submitter_type field was inferred heuristically from filenames and metadata rather than from an authoritative registry, which means some classifications (especially "Unknown") may be imprecise.
  • Topic model sensitivity. LDA coherence guided model selection, but coherence is an approximation of human interpretability, not a guarantee. Alternative topic counts or methods (e.g., BERTopic) might surface different thematic decompositions.

Recommended next steps¶

  1. Sentiment and stance analysis extend the pipeline with sentiment scoring or stance detection to measure not just what submitters discuss but how they feel about it (e.g., support vs. opposition to fixed term parliaments).
  2. Cross inquiry comparison apply the same pipeline to previous JSCEM inquiries (e.g., 2022 federal election) to track how public concerns evolve across electoral cycles.
  3. Transformer based topic modeling experiment with BERTopic or similar embedding based approaches to test whether contextual semantics produce more granular or interpretable topics.

7. References¶

Australian Parliament House. (n.d.). Making a submission to a committee inquiry. Inquiry into the 2025 federal election: Submissions. Retrieved March 9, 2026, from https://www.aph.gov.au/Parliamentary_Business/Committees/Joint/Electoral_Matters/2025federalelection/Submissions Australian Electoral Commission. (2025, August 11). Frequently asked questions. Retrieved March 9, 2026, from https://www.aec.gov.au/faqs/
